137	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
138	
139	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
140	
141	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
142	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:

147

10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.

13	## Skills

15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.

17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.

21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.

26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.

46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. 72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:

1	\chapter{The Grassmannian over $\mathbb{Z}$: affine charts and gluing}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianCells}
3	
4	\section{Set-up: charts indexed by $d$-subsets}
5	\label{sec:gr_setup}
6	
7	Fix integers \(r \ge d \ge 1\). For a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M\) over a ring
8	and a subset \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\), write \(M_I\)
9	for the \emph{\(I\)-th minor} of \(M\): the \(d \times d\) submatrix whose
10	columns are those indexed by \(I\). We construct the Grassmannian scheme
11	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing one affine chart \(U^I\) for
12	each such \(I\). The construction is, conceptually, the quotient
13	\(\mathrm{GL}_{d,\mathbb{Z}} \backslash V\) of the scheme \(V\) of rank-\(d\)
14	matrices, but we never use the language of group-scheme actions: the charts and
15	their transition maps are elementary matrix algebra over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Taking
16	\(d = 1\) recovers \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,1) = \mathbb{P}^{r-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\).
17	
18	\begin{definition}
19	
20	\leanok
21	[Affine chart \(U^I\)]
22	  \label{def:gr_affine_chart}
23	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.affineChart}
24	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
25	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
26	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L807-L821).
27	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
28	  % "If $M$ is a $d\times r$-matrix, and
29	  %  $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with cardinality $\#(I)$ equal
30	  %  to $d$, the $I$ th minor $M_I$ of $M$ will
31	  %  mean the $d\times d$ minor of $M$ whose columns are indexed by
32	  %  $I$.
33	  %
34	  %  For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$,
35	  %  consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
36	  %  $X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
37	  %  entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$.
38	  %  Let $\Z[X^I]$ denote the polynomial ring in the variables
39	  %  $x^I_{p,q}$, and let $U^I = \spec \Z[X^I]$, which is non-canonically
40	  %  isomorphic to the affine space $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$."
41	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
42	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). Let \(X^I\) denote the
43	  \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) is the \(d \times d\)
44	  identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\) and whose remaining \(d(r - d)\) entries are
45	  independent indeterminates \(x^I_{p,q}\) (\(1 \le p \le d\),
46	  \(q \in \{1,\dots,r\} \setminus I\)) over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Write
47	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) for the polynomial ring in these \(d(r-d)\) variables and
48	  set
49	  \[
50	    U^I \;:=\; \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I].
51	  \]
52	  The choice of an ordering of the free entries gives a (non-canonical)
53	  isomorphism \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\); in particular
54	  \(U^I\) is a smooth affine \(\mathbb{Z}\)-scheme of relative dimension
55	  \(d(r-d)\). Intuitively a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-point of \(U^I\) is a \(d \times r\)
56	  matrix whose \(I\)-columns form the identity, i.e.\ a rank-\(d\) quotient of
57	  \(\mathbb{Z}^{r}\) trivialised on the coordinates \(I\).
58	\end{definition}
59	
60	\section{Transition maps and the cocycle condition}
61	\label{sec:gr_transition}
62	
63	\subsection*{Mathlib inputs for the transition map}
64	
65	The construction of \(\theta_{I,J}\) is a Cramer-inverse computation over the
66	localised chart ring, followed by one application of the universal property of a
67	localisation. We record the four Mathlib facts it rests on as dependency
68	anchors.
69	
70	\begin{lemma}
71	[Localised element is a unit]
72	  \label{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
73	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.algebraMap_isUnit}
74	  \mathlibok
75	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
76	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(x \in R\), and \(S\) a localisation of \(R\)
77	  away from \(x\) (i.e.\ \(S = R[1/x]\)). Then the image of \(x\) under the
78	  structure map \(R \to S\) is a unit of \(S\).
79	\end{lemma}
80	
81	\begin{lemma}
82	[A square matrix is invertible iff its determinant is]
83	  \label{lem:mathlib_isUnit_iff_isUnit_det}
84	  \lean{Matrix.isUnit_iff_isUnit_det}
85	  \mathlibok
86	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
87	  For a square matrix \(A\) over a commutative ring, \(A\) is a unit of the
88	  matrix ring iff \(\det A\) is a unit of the scalar ring.
89	\end{lemma}
90	
91	\begin{lemma}
92	[Left Cramer inverse]
93	  \label{lem:mathlib_nonsing_inv_mul}
94	  \lean{Matrix.nonsing_inv_mul}
95	  \mathlibok
96	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
97	  For a square matrix \(A\) over a commutative ring with \(\det A\) a unit, the
98	  nonsingular inverse \(A^{-1}\) satisfies \(A^{-1} A = 1\).
99	\end{lemma}
100	
101	\begin{lemma}
102	[Right Cramer inverse]
103	  \label{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
104	  \lean{Matrix.mul_nonsing_inv}
105	  \mathlibok
106	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
107	  For a square matrix \(A\) over a commutative ring with \(\det A\) a unit, the
108	  nonsingular inverse \(A^{-1}\) satisfies \(A A^{-1} = 1\).
109	\end{lemma}
110	
111	\begin{lemma}
112	[Universal property of an away-localisation]
113	  \label{lem:mathlib_away_lift}
114	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.lift}
115	  \mathlibok
116	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
117	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(x \in R\), and \(S = R[1/x]\) a localisation
118	  of \(R\) away from \(x\). Given any ring homomorphism \(g : R \to P\) that
119	  sends \(x\) to a unit of \(P\), there is a unique ring homomorphism
120	  \(S \to P\) extending \(g\) along \(R \to S\).
121	\end{lemma}
122	
123	\subsection*{The transition map by matrix algebra}
124	
125	We now build \(\theta_{I,J}\) in seven steps over the localised chart ring
126	\(R^I_J := \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\), splitting at the matrix-algebra seams. The
127	ambient chart ring \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) is the polynomial ring of
128	\cref{def:gr_affine_chart} in the \(d(r-d)\) free variables \(x^I_{p,q}\)
129	(\(p \in \{1,\dots,d\}\), \(q \notin I\)).
130	
131	\begin{definition}
132	
133	\leanok
134	[The universal matrix \(X^I\)]
135	  \label{def:gr_universal_matrix}
136	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMatrix}
137	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart}
138	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
139	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
140	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L807-L821).
141	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
142	  % "For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$,
143	  %  consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
144	  %  $X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
145	  %  entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$."
146	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). With the chart ring
147	  %   `R^I := MvPolynomial (Fin d × {q : Fin r // q ∉ I}) ℤ`, define
148	  %   `universalMatrix d r I : Matrix (Fin d) (Fin r) R^I` whose entry at
149	  %   `(p, q)` is: the identity entry `if (orderIso I) p = q then 1 else 0`
150	  %   when `q ∈ I` (using the order isomorphism `Fin d ≃o I` from `#I = d`),
151	  %   and the indeterminate `MvPolynomial.X (p, ⟨q, hq⟩)` when `q ∉ I`.
152	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
153	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). The \emph{universal
154	  matrix} \(X^I\) is the \(d \times r\) matrix over the chart ring
155	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) whose \(I\)-minor \(X^I_I\) is the \(d \times d\) identity
156	  \(1_{d\times d}\) and whose remaining entries are the free indeterminates

159	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to A\) is the same as a \(d \times r\) matrix over \(A\)
160	  with identity \(I\)-minor.
161	\end{definition}

375	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, def:gr_minor_det, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_minorDet_unit}
376	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together

379	  % SOURCE QUOTE:

382	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target).
383	  %   `isUnit_transitionPreMap_minorDet d r I J :`
384	  %   `IsUnit (transitionPreMap d r I J (minorDet d r J I))`.
385	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
386	  The pre-localisation hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre})
387	  sends \(P^J_I\) (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}, the \(I\)-minor determinant of
388	  \(X^J\)) to a unit of \(R^I_J\). Indeed
389	  \[
390	    \tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I\bigr)
391	      = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr) = \det(X^I_J)^{-1} = 1/P^I_J,
392	  \]
393	  which is a unit.

395	\begin{proof}
396	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, def:gr_minor_det, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_minorDet_unit}
397	  \leanok

399	  X^I\), so \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) carries the \(I\)-minor \(X^J_I\) of \(X^J\) to
400	  the \(I\)-minor of \(M\), which is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) since
401	  the \(I\)-minor of \(X^I\) is the identity. Hence \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) =
402	  \det((X^I_J)^{-1}) = \det(X^I_J)^{-1}\). As \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit
403	  (\cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}), so is its inverse; the matrix-inverse
404	  determinant identity uses \cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}.
405	\end{proof}

478	\begin{lemma}
479	[Cocycle condition for the transition maps]
480	  \label{lem:gr_cocycle}
481	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.cocycleCondition}
482	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_transition_self}
483	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
484	  % affine patches" (read from
485	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L838-L848).
486	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
487	  % "Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$, and
488	  %  we leave it to the reader to verify that
489	  %  for any three subsets $I$, $J$ and $K$ of $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of
490	  %  cardinality $d$, the co-cycle condition
491	  %  $\theta_{I,K}= \theta_{I,J}\theta_{J,K}$
492	  %  is satisfied."
493	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
494	  For any three subsets \(I, J, K \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) of cardinality
495	  \(d\), the transition maps of \cref{def:gr_transition} satisfy the cocycle
496	  condition. As morphisms of schemes, on the triple overlap inside \(U^I\),
497	  \[
498	    \theta_{I,K} \;=\; \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J},
499	  \]
500	  equivalently, as ring homomorphisms,
501	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\) (the two read the same
502	  identity through the order-reversal of \(\Spec\)). Together with
503	  \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\), this is exactly the compatibility datum required to
504	  glue the charts.
505	\end{lemma}
506	
507	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$, and
508	% we leave it to the reader to verify that
509	% for any three subsets $I$, $J$ and $K$ of $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of
510	% cardinality $d$, the co-cycle condition
511	% $\theta_{I,K}= \theta_{I,J}\theta_{J,K}$
512	% is satisfied."  (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
513	% L838-L848.)
514	\begin{proof}
515	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_transition_self}
516	  We verify the scheme-morphism form
517	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) by a direct matrix
518	  computation, valid over the open locus of \(U^I\) on which all the minors
519	  involved are invertible (namely \(P^I_J\) and \(P^I_K\), which suffices since
520	  the maps are determined by their restriction to a dense open and all rings are
521	  domains; the computation is an identity of matrices with entries in
522	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J, 1/P^I_K]\)).
523	
524	  Start from the matrix \(X^I\) (the universal point of \(U^I\)). By
525	  \cref{def:gr_transition},
526	  \[
527	    M^J \;:=\; \theta_{I,J}(X^I) \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I,
528	  \]
529	  a \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(J\)-minor is the identity, hence a point of
530	  \(U^J\). Its \(K\)-minor is
531	  \[
532	    M^J_K \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_K,
533	    \qquad\text{so}\qquad
534	    (M^J_K)^{-1} \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I_J,
535	  \]
536	  using \((AB)^{-1} = B^{-1} A^{-1}\) for the invertible \(d \times d\) matrices
537	  \(X^I_J\) and \(X^I_K\). Applying \(\theta_{J,K}\) to \(M^J\) gives
538	  \[
539	    \theta_{J,K}(M^J) \;=\; (M^J_K)^{-1} M^J
540	    \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I_J \,\cdot\, (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I
541	    \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I,
542	  \]
543	  the middle product \(X^I_J (X^I_J)^{-1} = 1_{d\times d}\) cancelling by
544	  associativity of matrix multiplication. The right-hand side is precisely
545	  \(\theta_{I,K}(X^I) = (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I\). Thus
546	  \(\theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) and \(\theta_{I,K}\) agree on the
547	  universal matrix \(X^I\), hence agree as morphisms \(U^I \supseteq U^I_J \cap
548	  U^I_K \to U^K\). The special case \(K = I\) (resp.\ \(J = I\)) together with
549	  \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\) shows each \(\theta_{I,J}\) is an isomorphism onto its
550	  image with inverse \(\theta_{J,I}\), since
551	  \(\theta_{J,I} \circ \theta_{I,J} = \theta_{I,I} = \id\).
552	\end{proof}

554	\section{The glued Grassmannian scheme}
555	\label{sec:gr_glued}
556	
557	\begin{definition}
558	[The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
559	  \label{def:gr_glued_scheme}
560	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scheme}
561	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle}
562	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
563	  % affine patches" (read from
564	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L843-L850).
565	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
566	  % "Therefore the schemes
567	  %  $U^I$, as $I$ varies over all the
568	  %  ${r\choose d}$ different
569	  %  subsets of  $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of cardinality $d$,
570	  %  can be glued together by the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$ to form a
571	  %  finite-type scheme $\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$. As each $U^I$ is
572	  %  isomorphic to $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$, it follows that
573	  %  $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is smooth of relative dimension $d(r-d)$."
574	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
575	  Gluing the affine charts \(\{U^I\}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}), one for each
576	  of the \(\binom{r}{d}\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) of cardinality
577	  \(d\), along the open subschemes \(U^I_J \subseteq U^I\) via the transition
578	  isomorphisms \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I_J \xrightarrow{\sim} U^J_I\)
579	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}) -- which satisfy the cocycle condition
580	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) and \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\)
581	  of \cref{lem:gr_cocycle} -- yields a scheme
582	  \[
583	    \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \longrightarrow \Spec \mathbb{Z}.
584	  \]
585	  It is of finite type over \(\mathbb{Z}\) (a finite gluing of finitely
586	  generated \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebras), and under the gluing each \(U^I\) is an
587	  open subscheme of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with \(U^I \cap U^J = U^I_J\).
588	
589	  Moreover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is \emph{smooth of relative
590	  dimension \(d(r-d)\)}. Smoothness and relative dimension are local on the
591	  source; the charts \(\{U^I\}\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and each
592	  \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) is
593	  affine space of dimension \(d(r-d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\), which is smooth over
594	  \(\mathbb{Z}\) of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\) (the polynomial ring
595	  \(\mathbb{Z}[x_1, \dots, x_{d(r-d)}]\) is a smooth \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra). As
596	  the property may be checked on an open cover of the source, the conclusion
597	  follows.
598	\end{definition}

600	\section{Separatedness}
601	\label{sec:gr_separated}
602	
603	\begin{lemma}
604	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is separated over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
605	  \label{lem:gr_separated}
606	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isSeparated}
607	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_transition}
608	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Separatedness" (read from
609	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L856-L860).
610	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
611	  % "The intersection of the diagonal of $\grass(r,d)$ with $U^I\times U^J$
612	  %  can be seen to be the closed subscheme $\Delta_{I,J}\subset U^I\times U^J$
613	  %  defined by entries of the matrix formula $X^J_I\,X^I - X^J =0$,
614	  %  and so $\grass(r,d)$ is a separated scheme."
615	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
616	  The structure morphism \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)
617	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) is separated; equivalently, the diagonal
618	  \(\Delta : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \times_{\mathbb{Z}}
619	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is a closed immersion.
620	\end{lemma}
621	
622	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The intersection of the diagonal of $\grass(r,d)$ with
623	% $U^I\times U^J$ can be seen to be the closed subscheme
624	% $\Delta_{I,J}\subset U^I\times U^J$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
625	% $X^J_I\,X^I - X^J =0$, and so $\grass(r,d)$ is a separated scheme."
626	% (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L856-L860.)
627	\begin{proof}
628	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_transition}
629	  The products \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\), as \(I, J\) range over the
630	  size-\(d\) subsets, form an affine open cover of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)
631	  \times_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\). Separatedness may be checked on this
632	  cover: it suffices to show that for each pair \((I,J)\) the preimage
633	  \(\Delta^{-1}(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J)\) maps by a closed immersion into
634	  \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\), i.e.\ that the corresponding ring
635	  homomorphism is surjective.
636	
637	  By \cref{def:gr_glued_scheme} the diagonal preimage is the overlap
638	  \(\Delta^{-1}(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J) = U^I \cap U^J = U^I_J = \Spec
639	  \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\). The restricted diagonal corresponds to the ring map
640	  \[
641	    \delta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathbb{Z}[X^J]
642	    \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J],
643	    \qquad
644	    X^I \otimes 1 \mapsto X^I, \quad 1 \otimes X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I,
645	  \]
646	  the second component being the comorphism \(\theta_{I,J}\) of
647	  \cref{def:gr_transition} (a point of \(U^I_J\) and its image under
648	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) are the two coordinates of a point of the diagonal). We claim
649	  \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective. Its image contains all entries of \(X^I\)
650	  (from the first factor). From the second factor it contains the entries of
651	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\); taking the \(I\)-minor of this matrix, and using
652	  \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\), the image contains the entries of \((X^I_J)^{-1}\),
653	  and in particular
654	  \[
655	    \delta_{I,J}(1 \otimes P^J_I) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr) = 1/P^I_J .
656	  \]
657	  Hence \(1/P^I_J\) lies in the image, so the image is all of
658	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\) and \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective. Concretely the
659	  closed subscheme \(\Delta_{I,J} \subseteq U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\) is cut
660	  out by the entries of the matrix relation
661	  \[
662	    X^J_I \, X^I - X^J \;=\; 0,
663	  \]
664	  which on the diagonal holds because \(X^J = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) forces
665	  \(X^J_I = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) and therefore \(X^J_I X^I =
666	  (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I = X^J\). As each restricted diagonal is a closed immersion,
667	  \(\Delta\) is a closed immersion and \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is separated over
668	  \(\mathbb{Z}\).
669	\end{proof}

671	\section{Properness}
672	\label{sec:gr_proper}

733	\begin{proof}
734	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, lem:gr_separated, def:gr_transition}
735	  Since \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is of finite type
736	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) and separated (\cref{lem:gr_separated}), and
737	  \(\mathbb{Z}\) is Noetherian, properness follows from the valuative criterion
738	  for discrete valuation rings: it suffices to show that every solid diagram
739	  \[
740	    \begin{array}{ccc}
741	      \Spec K & \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } & \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \\
742	      \downarrow & & \downarrow \pi \\
743	      \Spec R & \longrightarrow & \Spec \mathbb{Z}
744	    \end{array}
745	  \]
746	  with \(R\) a discrete valuation ring, \(K = \operatorname{Frac}(R)\) and
747	  valuation \(\nu : K \to \mathbb{Z} \cup \{\infty\}\), admits a unique diagonal
748	  filler \(\Spec R \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\).
749	
750	  \emph{Existence.} The morphism \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) lands
751	  in some chart \(U^I\) (\(\Spec K\) has one point), so it is given by a ring map
752	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\). Among the \(\binom{r}{d}\) minors choose \(J\)
753	  with \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) minimal. Because \(P^I_I = 1\) gives \(f(P^I_I) = 1\)
754	  with \(\nu = 0\), the minimum satisfies \(\nu(f(P^I_J)) \le 0\); in particular
755	  \(f(P^I_J) \ne 0\), so \(f(X^I_J) \in \mathrm{GL}_d(K)\) is invertible over
756	  \(K\). Define
757	  \[
758	    g : \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \longrightarrow K, \qquad
759	    g(X^J) = f\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\bigr) ;
760	  \]
761	  this is the composite of \(\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}) with
762	  \(f\), so \(g\) defines the same \(K\)-point \(\varphi\), now exhibited as
763	  landing in \(U^J\).
764	
765	  We show every entry \(g(x^J_{p,q})\) lies in \(R\), i.e.\ has \(\nu \ge 0\).
766	  First, for any size-\(d\) subset \(K' \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) the \(K'\)-minor
767	  of \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_{K'}\), so
768	  \[
769	    g(P^J_{K'}) = \det f\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_{K'}\bigr)
770	    = \frac{f(P^I_{K'})}{f(P^I_J)},
771	    \qquad
772	    \nu(g(P^J_{K'})) = \nu(f(P^I_{K'})) - \nu(f(P^I_J)) \ge 0
773	  \]
774	  by minimality of \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\). Now the \(J\)-minor \(X^J_J\) of \(X^J\) is
775	  the identity, so by cofactor expansion each individual entry
776	  \(x^J_{p,q}\) (with \(q \notin J\)) equals, up to sign, the minor
777	  \(P^J_{K'}\) for \(K' = (J \setminus \{j_p\}) \cup \{q\}\) -- the \(d \times d\)
778	  determinant obtained by replacing the \(p\)-th identity column of \(X^J_J\) by
779	  the column \(q\). Hence \(\nu(g(x^J_{p,q})) = \nu(g(P^J_{K'})) \ge 0\) for all
780	  entries, and the entries with \(q \in J\) are \(0\) or \(1\). Therefore
781	  \(g(\mathbb{Z}[X^J]) \subseteq R\), so \(g\) factors uniquely as
782	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to R \hookrightarrow K\). The corresponding morphism
783	  \(\Spec R \to U^J \hookrightarrow \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) restricts to \(\varphi\)
784	  over \(\Spec K\) and lies over \(\Spec \mathbb{Z}\), providing the filler.
785	
786	  \emph{Uniqueness.} Any two fillers agree over the generic point \(\Spec K\)
787	  and, \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) being separated over \(\mathbb{Z}\)
788	  (\cref{lem:gr_separated}), two morphisms \(\Spec R \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\)
789	  agreeing on the schematically dense open \(\Spec K\) coincide. This verifies
790	  the valuative criterion, so \(\pi : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is
791	  proper.
792	\end{proof}

794	\section{Out of scope}
795	\label{sec:gr_out_of_scope}
796	
797	This chapter constructs the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over
798	the absolute base \(\mathbb{Z}\) and establishes its smoothness, separatedness
799	and properness. The following constructions are deferred to later chapters and
800	are not treated here:
801	\begin{itemize}
802	  \item \textbf{The tautological/universal quotient} \(u : \mathcal{O}^{\oplus
803	    r}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) and the determinant
804	    line bundle \(\det \mathcal{U}\) with its Pl\"ucker embedding.
805	  \item \textbf{The functor-of-points representability} of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\)
806	    -- that it represents \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d) =
807	    \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^r \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}} /
808	    \mathbb{Z} / \mathbb{Z}}\); this is blueprinted as
809	    \cref{thm:grassmannian_representable} in
810	    \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}.
811	  \item \textbf{The relative version over a general base \(S\)} with a locally
812	    free sheaf \(V\) of rank \(r\): the scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V,d)\) obtained by
813	    base change and gluing over a trivialising cover of \(V\). The
814	    absolute-over-\(\mathbb{Z}\) construction of this chapter is the input to
815	    that base change.
816	\end{itemize}

  neither Lean proof uses it (see below). (must-fix F-4b)

## F-4a — the `% LEAN SIGNATURE` I pinned for `cocycleCondition`

**Composition direction.** RING-HOM direction `θ_{I,K} = θ_{I,J} ∘ θ_{J,K}`
(the scheme-morphism reading `θ_{J,K} ∘ θ_{I,J}` is its Spec-dual). This is the
direction consistent with the landed `transitionMap` orientation
(read from `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/GrassmannianCells.lean:245`):

```
transitionMap d r I J hI hJ :
  Localization.Away (minorDet d r J I hJ hI) →+* Localization.Away (minorDet d r I J hI hJ)
```

i.e. domain `R^J[1/P^J_I]`, codomain `R^I[1/P^I_J]`.

**The genuine typing subtlety (the iter-011 prover's flag), now resolved in the
signature.** The naive
`transitionMap I K = (transitionMap I J).comp (transitionMap J K)` does **not**
typecheck:
- `transitionMap J K` codomain `Localization.Away (minorDet d r J K hJ hK)` (`R^J[1/P^J_K]`)
  ≠ `transitionMap I J` domain `Localization.Away (minorDet d r J I hJ hI)` (`R^J[1/P^J_I]`);
- `transitionMap I K` domain `Localization.Away (minorDet d r K I hK hI)` (`R^K[1/P^K_I]`)
  ≠ the composite's start `Localization.Away (minorDet d r K J hK hJ)` (`R^K[1/P^K_J]`).

The two `IsLocalization.Away.lift` codomains/domains differ, so I pinned the
conclusion over the **triple-overlap rings** obtained by inverting BOTH relevant
minors (named explicitly in the signature so the prover knows the formalization shape):

```
S_K := Localization.Away (minorDet d r K I hK hI * minorDet d r K J hK hJ)  -- R^K[1/(P^K_I P^K_J)]
S_J := Localization.Away (minorDet d r J I hJ hI * minorDet d r J K hJ hK)  -- R^J[1/(P^J_I P^J_K)]
S_I := Localization.Away (minorDet d r I J hI hJ * minorDet d r I K hI hK)  -- R^I[1/(P^I_J P^I_K)]
```

with the canonical `algebraMap`s from each single away-localisation into the matching
double one, giving localised transition maps
`Θ_{J,K} : S_K →+* S_J`, `Θ_{I,J} : S_J →+* S_I`, `Θ_{I,K} : S_K →+* S_I` (each the
`IsLocalization.Away.lift`/`IsLocalization.lift` of the relevant `transitionPreMap`
composed with the structure map into the double localisation). The pinned conclusion:

```
cocycleCondition d r I J K (hI : I.card = d) (hJ : J.card = d) (hK : K.card = d) :
  Θ_{I,K} = Θ_{I,J}.comp Θ_{J,K}      -- propositional equality of ring homs  S_K →+* S_I
```

The three cardinality hypotheses `hI hJ hK : _.card = d` are spelled out. The note
that pinning over the chart ring `S_I` on the triple-overlap open is acceptable (it
matches the prose proof, which computes over `ℤ[X^I, 1/P^I_J, 1/P^I_K] = S_I`) is
included in the comment.

The existing proof sketch is consistent with this signature (it checks agreement of
the two ring homs on the K-generators `x^K_{p,q}`, both giving `(X^I_K)^{-1} X^I` over
`S_I`); per the directive I did **not** rewrite the sketch.

I read both Lean proofs:
- `isUnit_det_universalMinor` (`lem:gr_minorDet_unit`,

A repo-wide grep (`grep -rn isUnit_iff_isUnit_det AlgebraicJacobian/`) returned no hits.
Since neither proof (nor any other Lean decl) uses it, per the directive I removed the
orphaned `\mathlibok` anchor block entirely.

- `leandag build --json`: `unknown_uses: []` (no broken `\uses` introduced by the removal).

  nodes remain in the chapter; the previously-isolated `lem:mathlib_isUnit_iff_isUnit_det`
  is gone, and `lem:gr_cocycle` is wired in both directions —

- The `\mathlibok` Cramer-inverse anchors `lem:mathlib_nonsing_inv_mul` and

## References consulted
None (no new citation block authored). The `% SOURCE`/`% SOURCE QUOTE` material on the
cocycle block was already present from prior work (Nitsure §1); I added only the
`% LEAN SIGNATURE` engineering comment and removed one Mathlib anchor — neither requires
a source quote.

- **Formalization-shape note for the cocycle prover (not strategy-modifying).** The

  prover must first build the three doubly-localised rings `S_K, S_J, S_I` and the
